words: SAM SCHREGER: was it a long trip?
NONA WILKINS LAWRENCE: over the road.

speaker: SS
words: How long, I wonder, did it take you?

speaker: NWL
words: Oh, my, started early in the morning, and we didn't get here wil way

speaker: NWL
words: in the afternoon.

speaker: SS
words: Nora.

speaker: NWL
words: Yes, there was a store there. A store, and I don't know what else.

speaker: NWL
words: When we got there it was just a place to stop and feed our horses

speaker: NWL
words: and have our lunches.

speaker: SS
words: You were just little kids then.

speaker: NWL
words: I was eight years old.

speaker: SS
words: And you kept asking how long it was going to take you?

speaker: NWL
words: Yes, because I was getting tired.

speaker: SS
words: He lied to you, when he said it was only going to take a little while.

speaker: NWL
words: And then we got over here to where the old saloon building was, where

speaker: NWL
words: his folks lived and I said how much farther; "Oh, it's about half way."

speaker: NWL
words: And I thought..

speaker: SS
words: Where did you kids ride? Did you ride in the back?

speaker: NWL
words: No, right up in the seat with him, right along with him.

speaker: SS
words: What was the store like? What did they sell?

speaker: SS
words: FLOYD LAWRENCE: Groceries, mostly.

speaker: FL
words: I was just a kid when I saw that and I couldn't figure that all out.

speaker: SS
words: Where was that?

speaker: FL
words: It was down on Bear Creek. right there on Bear Creek.

speaker: NWL
words: That was the one that Arthur told us about.

speaker: SS
words: Did you know Mary and Lou Wells?

speaker: SS
words: What do you remember them being like? The Wells. I've heard quite

speaker: SS
words: a lot of things about them. Most of them really good.

speaker: NWL
words: She was a wonderful person. She was just a real good person.

speaker: SS
words: yeah, the boys. I was talking to? I don't think you ever knew

speaker: SS
words: the Settles down there. They were black. Down there in Moscow, al

speaker: SS
words: though the one, Gene Settles, he's still living.

speaker: FL
words: The one that was here, that family, you know the people mixed with

speaker: FL
words: them just the same, as they did white people.

speaker: NWL
words: Well, they were.more like white people, you know, because they were

speaker: NWL
words: the only family among the white people at that time, the colored peo

speaker: NWL
words: ple, they were colored and the rest were white and they went with the

speaker: NWL
words: white kids to school, and naturally, they kind of become more white,

speaker: NWL
words: acted more white. And Mary stayed with me, before she went to Spokane.

speaker: NWL
words: She was our neighbor in later years, Mary Kay was.

speaker: SS
words: When did she go to Spokane? When did she leave the area? Was it before

speaker: SS
words: the second World War?

speaker: FL
words: It was way afterwards-

speaker: NWL
words: Yeah, it was afterwards, because she had this building brought down

speaker: NWL
words: and rebuilt down here.

speaker: NWL
words: Gene Settle was telling me about the guy that she had been married

speaker: NWL
words: to from Spokane during World War I.

speaker: NWL
words: That was ") that was the father to Lou Easter and Bill. And he did,

speaker: NWL
words: he disappeared.

speaker: NWL
words: He was a army officer, pretty well educated Nigger. And didn the

speaker: NWL
words: make suits and stuff? Wasn't that his trade?

speaker: NWL
words: He was a tailor, her husband. Had a tailor shop in But

speaker: NWL
words: they didn't get along too well. After these children was born, he

speaker: NWL
words: just disappeared, he just left her.

speaker: SS
words: That made it pretty hard on her.

speaker: NWL
words: Well, it did, but she had been used to- she was like her mother, she

speaker: NWL
words: was used to working. And she did housework for people and she washed,

speaker: NWL
words: took in washing and so she made a living for the children. And of

speaker: NWL
words: course, Roy, her brother would stay there with her and I suppose he

speaker: NWL
words: paid her board, too.

speaker: SS
words: What about Chuck? Didn't he work in the mills?

speaker: FL
words: Yeah.

speaker: SS
words: Now, I've heard- some people have said that Joe was really strong, a

speaker: SS
words: very strong man and some people have said that it was Chuck that really

speaker: SS
words: the strong man.

speaker: NWL
words: No, it was Joe.

speaker: FL
words: Joe was liked better than any man in this country. He'd get in a rig

speaker: FL
words: and go to town and everybody, would just wave.

speaker: NWL
words: Oh, they were well liked.

speaker: SS
words: Where did Joe get this reputation for being a strong man?

speaker: FL
words: He held up for his rights, Joe did, a lot more than Chuck. But they

speaker: FL
words: wasn't either one outstanding that way, I don't think. I'll have to

speaker: FL
words: tell you what happened one time. They's a bunch of natural meadows

speaker: FL
words: up above here, and Iand him was up there mowing hay, and Ihad anew

speaker: FL
words: mower and he had an old one. His would keep breaking down all time

speaker: FL
words: and it was real hot and Iwas helping him get his mower fixed. I

speaker: FL
words: was ahead of him and I'd made around and come up to him and stopped,

speaker: FL
words: see, and that wild grass is real hard to cut, you have to have an aw

speaker: FL
words: ful good mower to cut it. So, Iwas laying out on the grass there

speaker: FL
words: pretty close to it, helping him fix it and Isaid to him, "Joe, what

speaker: FL
words: makes you smell like that when you get hot?" He said, "You smell

speaker: FL
words: that way to me, too!" Isaid, "Well, I'll get away from you then!"

speaker: FL
words: (Chuckles) He was quite an old character, now, I'll tell you.

speaker: FL
words: He was astrong, to be acolored person and him the only family in

speaker: FL
words: the country, he was astrong person that way. He held up for his

speaker: FL
words: rights and he was ahard worker and he worked in the woods and he

speaker: FL
words: hauled freight and just astrong character that way, physically.

speaker: FL
words: What about Lou? Gene Settle was telling me, that Lou didn't care

speaker: FL
words: very much for some of the harder, you know, the drinking ways.

speaker: FL
words: She was a very woman that way.

speaker: FL
words: And he was pretty bad to drink.

speaker: NWL
words: No, she didn't go in for that and she had ahard life that way, be

speaker: NWL
words: cause he drank quite heavy, and she was such a good, kind soul.

speaker: NWL
words: Gene said she really took it in silence, and did her work and always

speaker: NWL
words: kept food on the table.

speaker: NWL
words: And she did, she was a good cook.

speaker: NWL
words: You know, they still remember in Troy, the old-timers do, tell about

speaker: NWL
words: how Joe used to come into Troy and go drinking. There acouple of

speaker: NWL
words: stories about him standing up to- Idon't know if you ever heard of

speaker: NWL
words: Marshall Hays- Hays is this guy that got shot in Troy about 1904.

speaker: NWL
words: We lived just below where he was shot, there just below the water

speaker: NWL
words: tank, at that time

speaker: SS
words: Oh, really?

speaker: NWL
words: That must have been when?

speaker: SS
words: 1904 was the year, that's the way I understand it.

speaker: NWL
words: I know we was awful scared, all of us, you know, we was little kids

speaker: NWL
words: and that was going on. We could see right there, but of course,

speaker: NWL
words: I was too small to realize.

speaker: SS
words: You were there when it happened?

speaker: NWL
words: Oh, yes, we lived right down there when it happened. Now let me see,

speaker: NWL
words: He was shot by whom Frei? No. Jake Sly.

speaker: SS
words: Right.

speaker: NWL
words: Well, he was shot.

speaker: SS
words: It was Sly that did the shooting, I thought.

speaker: NWL
words: Was it? Yes, Jake lived by us, yes, Jake Sly lived by us, right next

speaker: NWL
words: door. And I think he was a brother, if I remember right. And, I ,

speaker: NWL
words: the people in Troy was really mad and they got their guns out and just

speaker: NWL
words: about ready to kill him.

speaker: SS
words: Sly, you mean.

speaker: NWL
words: Yeah, this Sly. But then they arrested him and that was it.

speaker: SS
words: But then on the other hand, I've heard that Hays was not at all well

speaker: SS
words: liked, and a lot of people thought he was mean.

speaker: NWL
words: Well, I guess he really was, I don't know.

speaker: NWL
words: (tape was stopped at this point)

speaker: SS
words: He looked the situation over.

speaker: NWL
words: Yes, he was up here and looked it all over, the store was here then,

speaker: NWL
words: a good

speaker: NWL
words: and they thought it would be.business because they was putting the

speaker: NWL
words: railroad through. Of course, those men, they kind of like to get out

speaker: NWL
words: you know after they got their checks and so they would rent the horses

speaker: NWL
words: or Bovill

speaker: NWL
words: then- or hire him to take 'em to Troy or wherever they wanted to go.

speaker: SS
words: Was that the mam was the lumber-

speaker: NWL
words: jacks and the people building the railroads?

speaker: NWL
words: Yes, and the men that was working on the railroad.

speaker: FL
words: Well, at that time there was trees all around here.

speaker: NWL
words: See, here where they've cut these stumps here. And then that was

speaker: NWL
words: the Grammis house. Wasn't a very big house- now what was this?

speaker: FL
words: That's the house.

speaker: FL
words: I can't figure who built that. Just turn it around- now this is

speaker: FL
words: the way it sat. And we went to school to her.

speaker: FL
words: Anna Marie- yes, I remember her mentioning you when I talked to her.

speaker: FL
words: She sure remembers you.

speaker: FL
words: Who is that?

speaker: SS
words: Anna Marie Oslund. She taught before she was eighteen, a little before she was

speaker: SS
words: eighteen.

speaker: SS
words: When you were going to school?

speaker: SS
words: Really? You were in one of her first classes? She was very young then,

speaker: NWL
words: Yes, she wasn't quite eighteen. She couldn't collect her wages, her

speaker: NWL
words: dad came and would collect 'em. He took 'em, too.

speaker: SS
words: What do you remember about that, when she was teaching school?

speaker: NWL
words: That was her first, and then she came back a few years later

speaker: NWL
words: and she taught another- what two terms?

speaker: NWL
words: Oslund was her name then.

speaker: NWL
words: Well, that was after she was married. that was her

speaker: NWL
words: maiden name.

speaker: NWL
words: Who taught with her?

speaker: NWL
words: Fairbanks. The second time she taught, well, that's

speaker: NWL
words: when I went to school to her.

speaker: SS
words: Do you remember the school term when she was here, teaching?

speaker: FL
words: Oh, yeah.

speaker: NWL
words: What year?

speaker: SS
words: I'm not thinking of the year so much as what it was like.

speaker: NWL
words: Oh, yes, I should say. I tell you, she was an outdoor person. She'd

speaker: NWL
words: get on her skis and go over these fences, take a bunch of the boys,

speaker: NWL
words: you know and go over these fences and this old bachelor down here he

speaker: NWL
words: said that she broke his fences down. He got kind of mad at her, you

speaker: NWL
words: know.

speaker: LL
words: She always took the boys.

speaker: NWL
words: Oh, yes!

speaker: LL
words: The girls didn't amount to much!

speaker: NWL
words: And this was the first train.

speaker: SS
words: First train? Oh.

speaker: NWL
words: We all went over.

speaker: SS
words: And that was the one that came from Potlatch; all the brass and all?

speaker: NWL
words: Yes.

speaker: SS
words: And they all came up to Bovill? Came all the way to Bovill?

speaker: NWL
words: I don't know whether they was there that day or not.

speaker: FL
words: I don't know as they was, but that was the main track that run to

speaker: FL
words: Bovill.

speaker: SS
words: Everybody went out to look at that?

speaker: NWL
words: Oh, yes, my, that was something! That was the new railroad through

speaker: NWL
words: here. You know that was something to have a railroad coming up

speaker: NWL
words: through here because they'd been traveling with just horses and wagons

speaker: LL
words: And the roads were terrible.

speaker: NWL
words: Yes. Why yes, it was so narrow that the limbs'd hit you, you know,

speaker: NWL
words: when you was riding along. It was that kind of roads.

speaker: SS
words: Did the train stop in Helmer, and did they have any ceremonies or

speaker: SS
words: anything?

speaker: NWL
words: No, no, they just- people went over there to see it go through, that

speaker: NWL
words: was just an occasion, you know. My mother's in there, and I think

speaker: NWL
words: Floyd's mother, and it's so faint you can't see it, of course.

speaker: SS
words: Had your father been a- did he run a livery barn before? Or was this

speaker: SS
words: his first one?

speaker: NWL
words: No. No. We came to Troy in 1900, and he had a dray. He bought out

speaker: NWL
words: the dray business there, and he rented the teams and then he went into

speaker: NWL
words: partnership with Ben Nelson and a feed store and then he had a little

speaker: NWL
words: confectionery store, later on. And he contracted typhoid fever and

speaker: NWL
words: he had to give his store up. And then of course, he lost his foot, it

speaker: NWL
words: settled in his foot and he had to have it amputated. And then his

speaker: NWL
words: next deal was up here with the livery stable.

speaker: FL
words: And he had one eye: didn't he get that cut in a corn patch?

speaker: NWL
words: Yeah, he fell and he could see, but the lid was drawn down and in those

speaker: NWL
words: days they didn't do anything about it, you know, when it could have

speaker: NWL
words: been fixed, because he could see out of it, but he couldn't lift his

speaker: NWL
words: lid.

speaker: SS
words: Did it happen to him here, or was it back before he came?

speaker: NWL
words: Pardon?

speaker: SS
words: Did that happen in Idaho?

speaker: NWL
words: Back in Kentucky, before he came here.

speaker: SS
words: Did the family come straight here from Kentucky?

speaker: NWL
words: To Troy.

speaker: SS
words: Did they know people in Troy?

speaker: NWL
words: His father was there. His father had come ahead of him, see and his

speaker: NWL
words: family. And so then they just thought he'd make a better living out

speaker: NWL
words: here, and so they moved to Troy. That was in 1900. And my granddad,

speaker: NWL
words: Wilkins, was already here at that time, he and his family.

speaker: SS
words: Are you related to Ken Wilkins?

speaker: NWL
words: Yes, cousin to Ken. And then my mother's folks came later on, the

speaker: NWL
words: Leads, they came out, and they lived in Troy.

speaker: SS
words: Did your parents meet in Troy, then?

speaker: NWL
words: Pardon?

speaker: SS
words: Did your parents meet in Troy, or had they gotten married back

speaker: NWL
words: No, they were married when they came out. Yes, there was four of us

speaker: NWL
words: kids in our family, the Wilkins family. Edgar Wilkins family.

speaker: NWL
words: Now, you'd better tell him something about your family.

speaker: SS
words: Yeah, your family was- weren't they about the first family? Was it

speaker: SS
words: at McGary Meadows that they were the first family?

speaker: FL
words: My granddad; I don't think he ever proved up on McGary Meadows, but

speaker: FL
words: he squatted on that and built a house on that. And I think he sold

speaker: FL
words: it and he left here and went up to Nez Perce when they opened up that

speaker: FL
words: reservation. And he was a funny guy; he would go into a place and

speaker: FL
words: fix it up just so he could live good, somebody come along and buy it

speaker: FL
words: and he'd go somewheres else, and start all over again. I never figured

speaker: FL
words: that out.

speaker: SS
words: I've read that a lot of guys did that in the early- the early pioneers

speaker: SS
words: did that a lot, then they'd move on.

speaker: NWL
words: Well, he was a carpenter, too, wasn't he?

speaker: FL
words: Yeah he was a natural born-

speaker: NWL
words: He was a good carpenter.

speaker: SS
words: Maybe he liked to build houses.

speaker: NWL
words: I think he did.

speaker: SS
words: Build himself a new one.

speaker: NWL
words: Well, the last one he built, he built down on, what was it? The

speaker: NWL
words: Sweet Water?

speaker: FL
words: Yeah.

speaker: NWL
words: And part of it still stands.

speaker: SS
words: The Sweet Water in Wyoming?

speaker: NWL
words: No, down here by Lapwai. Up that way.

speaker: SS
words: When he was down here, were his kids here at the same time with him?

speaker: SS
words: Your father, was he here, too?

speaker: NWL
words: They moved from Iowa, didn't they? Not straight to Idaho.

speaker: FL
words: No, they moved to Dayton.

speaker: NWL
words: Down around Dayton.

speaker: FL
words: And they stayed there a while and then they come up here in- what was

speaker: SS
words: Was your grandfather gone by then?

speaker: FL
words: No.

speaker: SS
words: Was he still here when your father came?

speaker: FL
words: Well, the whole family come together. There wa,s, I don't know, Dad

speaker: FL
words: had about four brothers and two sisters and they all come. And down

speaker: FL
words: here- this guy that bought my dad's place- they stayed there- well,

speaker: FL
words: my dad stayed there the first winter and then my granddad he went on

speaker: FL
words: Pp to Mc Gary Meadows. And he built a house there,a pretty nice house,

speaker: FL
words: and then he sold it. Sold his rights to Mc Gary.

speaker: SS
words: I wonder, when he came to McGary Meadows there must have hardly been

speaker: SS
words: anybody there yet; was there? It must have been pretty empty, around

speaker: SS
words: there.

speaker: FL
words: There wasn't no other families there. And I think there was a family

speaker: FL
words: lived at the forks of Potlatch up here. What was their names?

speaker: NWL
words: Carlsons.

speaker: FL
words: Carlsons.

speaker: SS
words: Can you figure why people hadn't moved up in there before, when in

speaker: SS
words: 1900 it was still so empty?

speaker: FL
words: Well, it was all covered with timber and there wasn't much of a way

speaker: FL
words: to make a living. And my dad said when he come here, you could drive

speaker: FL
words: a team of horses anywheres you wanted to go around this country, it

speaker: FL
words: was all clean, see.

speaker: NWL
words: The underbrush was gone.

speaker: FL
words: Them Indians kept it cleared off. And they kept it burnt close enough

speaker: FL
words: so that it didn't make a hot enough fire to kill the trees. And I

speaker: FL
words: know my dad said that if he'd a burnt, he'd a burnt it all-

speaker: SS
words: What did your father do then? Did he build himself a place, too?

speaker: FL
words: Well, the whole family lived there one winter and they built this

speaker: FL
words: house and then they went on, see and Dad stayed there. I said to my

speaker: FL
words: dad one time, "How come you didn't gov to the reservation, that's

speaker: FL
words: good soil over there." "Well," he said, "son, I was just so poor I

speaker: FL
words: couldn't go." (Chuckles)

speaker: FL
words: How old was he about then? Was he grown?

speaker: FL
words: Oh, yeah. He was twenty-one when he got here.

speaker: FL
words: So what did he do then to make a living? After they went?

speaker: FL
words: well, he went out in th0 country and worked in the harvest.

speaker: FL
words: He was a sacksewer and he done that for years. And us kids and Mother

speaker: FL
words: stayed there at home. But that's the way they got by the winters.

speaker: SS
words: He could make enough money seweing sacks to support the family for

speaker: SS
words: the rest of the year?

speaker: FL
words: Uh-huh.

speaker: SS
words: Must not have taken too much.

speaker: FL
words: Well, it's a lot different now.

speaker: NWL
words: Well, they accumulated a little more stock, too, to help along.

speaker: FL
words: Oh, yeah.

speaker: NWL
words: Some cows and they just gradually built up. Just like any home

speaker: NWL
words: steader.

speaker: SS
words: Was he homesteading then, or did he get the land from somebody?

speaker: FL
words: No, he homesteaded it.

speaker: NWL
words: It was 1890, tells us. And I think our papers are home.

speaker: NWL
words: I have all this at home. 1890 when the Lawrences came. See, I got

speaker: NWL
words: this from partly from this book. And I just scribbled

speaker: NWL
words: it down because he told us about it. And I helped Miller go around

speaker: NWL
words: and get some of this, you know.

speaker: SS
words: Oh, I didn't realize this because both your names are in here quite

speaker: SS
words: a bit.

speaker: NWL
words: There's one or two mistakes in there, but in my book, I've corrected

speaker: NWL
words: them.

speaker: SS
words: It's inevitable. You've got to make mistakes, you don't have a chance

speaker: SS
words: to check with everybody. I thought he did a real good job writing

speaker: SS
words: this book, myself.

speaker: FL
words: He did.

speaker: NWL
words: He did, and he tried to get the real facts as near as he could. And

speaker: NWL
words: he was very careful.

speaker: FL
words: He was at our house there for two weeks, straight.

speaker: SS
words: Were you living up here then, or was it down there?

speaker: FL
words: No, we lived here. We've been down there three years. There's the house

speaker: FL
words: that my mother lived in- that log house.

speaker: NWL
words: Now this is a family that lived up there on McGary Butte. That was

speaker: NWL
words: Hampton Phelps. And see the store- that's the only store that was

speaker: NWL
words: here, was the Janesville store and his folks ran it. These home

speaker: NWL
words: steaders would have to walk from those homesteads clear down here to

speaker: NWL
words: get groceries. Some of 'em didn't have horses. And it would take

speaker: NWL
words: 'em all day to walk down from up there on McGary Butte down to the

speaker: NWL
words: store; to his store.

speaker: FL
words: I'll never forget, his wife- this old Phelps wasn't very well,

speaker: FL
words: he's was kind of a fat guy, and his wife was a little old skinny wo

speaker: FL
words: man and she come down to my dad and she wanted some groceries; he

speaker: FL
words: said, "No," he said, "I can't sell you any."

speaker: FL
words: And you know, that woman just stood there and cried.

speaker: NWL
words: And that was hard for anybody to tell fem that, too.

speaker: SS
words: What did your father say then?

speaker: FL
words: Well, he finally let her have enough to get by on.

speaker: SS
words: When your family there- when you were a kid at McGary Meadows- where

speaker: SS
words: was this homestead? Was it right at Janesville?

speaker: FL
words: Yeah.

speaker: SS
words: Right where he later built the store?

speaker: FL
words: Yeah, that's where the store was.

speaker: NWL
words: He had the post office. And he'd bring the mail up from Troy by team.

speaker: NWL
words: And that was the center post office. They would get their mail thereall

speaker: NWL
words: these homesteaders did from there.

speaker: SS
words: So that was, in other words, that was before there was anything at

speaker: SS
words: Helmer?

speaker: NWL
words: Oh, yes.

speaker: SS
words: He started with a homestead; he didn't have the store right away.

speaker: NWL
words: Oh, no. Not when he had to go out and work, you know, to support the

speaker: NWL
words: family. It just kept building up. Now this is your picture there.

speaker: NWL
words: This is our picture we have taken from the other view. This is all

speaker: NWL
words: the people, most of em. !'"

speaker: FL
words: They're all gone now. Every one of 'em.

speaker: SS
words: Well, Marvin Miller is in here in this picture. May Wilkins is in

speaker: SS
words: here, too.

speaker: NWL
words: Now this isn't McGary. He was the father of Jim, owned what's

speaker: NWL
words: called McGary Meadows. And this is my brother. Now they made a mis

speaker: NWL
words: take, this is Floyd Lawrence, but that was my brother Lloyd Wilkins.
